learning_outcomes.description: Appropriately idealise the geometry of aerospace structural components subject to external applied loading, and calculate section properties and internal loading using standard analytical techniques for thin-walled structures.
Predict the limit and ultimate strength of standard aerospace structural members, including columns, stiffeners, unstiffened and stiffened panels, and fastened joints.
Use aerospace structural analysis techniques to perform preliminary sizing of an aerospace structural assembly and certify the new design in an engineering report.
Identify and explain the characteristics, performance requirements, loading conditions, and mechanical property requirements of metallic materials used in aerospace vehicles.

Identify and quantify the relationships between material composition, processing, microstructure, and properties, and explain the manufacturing processes used to produce aerospace structural components.

Integrate knowledge of aerospace materials, manufacturing processes, and structural requirements to conduct material selection, and work in a team to design manufacturing solutions for advanced aerospace applications.

assessments.weighting: <dd>10% (max 1% per week; total mark calculated by adding the 10 best weekly marks) </dd>
assessments.task_description:
• The teaching staff will answer questions and explain the task to be completed during the applied class.
  • Solutions to some or all of the applied class problems will be presented and discussed during the course of the applied class.


The maximum mark which can be obtained for this item of assessment is 10%, which is calculated by adding together your 10 best marks during the course of the semester. For example: if you submit 9 quizzes during the course of the semester and receive full marks for each, your final mark for this item of assessment will be 9%; if you submit 6 quizzes which get full marks, and 6 quizzes which receive 50% of full mark, your final mark for this item of assessment will be 6*1 + 4*0.5 = 8%; if you submit 12 quizzes during the course of the semester and receive full marks for each, your final mark for this item of assessment will be 10%.

Please refer to Blackboard for a detailed marking criteria.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submit an electronic copy of your working via Blackboard (\\ Assessment \\ applied classes \\ applied class Week X).
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
In this assignment students will design a minimum weight aircraft structural component/assembly and certify the structural integrity of their design.

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance.

Please refer to Blackboard for detailed marking criteria.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
This assignment is centred around design and testing of a high strength aerospace aluminium alloy, and there are two associated laboratory sessions.

The initial phase of this assignment is to be performed in groups. Students will design a heat treatment process for an aluminium alloy in order to achieve specified mechanical properties. In the first laboratory session, supplied tensile and compressive test specimens will be subjected to the designed heat treatment schedules. In the second laboratory session the heat treated samples will be mechanically tested, providing tensile and compressive stress-strain data.

Students will then submit individual reports on this work, on the material design process and experiments, including background discussion. As part of this analysis, students will use stress-strain data from the laboratory experiments to derive mechanical properties for the alloy.

If, for whatever reason, you find that your group is not functioning effectively, please contact your Course Coordinator for support.

You must attend the laboratory sessions to receive all available marks for this item of assessment. If you cannot attend the practical then you need to apply for an extension. This may result in you either being allocated to a new practical time, or else being approved to submit the assessment subject to alternative arrangements, to be determined by the Course Coordinator. Where a student fails to attend their group's practicals without an approved reason, “Experimental Procedure”, “Results and Discussion” and “Conclusions” sections of their assignment will be marked as zero (where they were absent from both practicals) or else each mark against these criteria will be reduced by 50% (where they were absent from one practical).

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance.

Please refer to Blackboard for detailed marking criteria.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

learning_activities.topic:
Applied Classes (Weeks 1-10, 12-13)

This time is set aside for solving example problems, and includes time to ask questions about the course content and the assignments. Problems will be issued to students before the scheduled applied class time. The teaching staff will answer questions and explain the task to be completed individually during the applied class session. Students are actively encouraged to form in groups during the applied class and collaborate with group members in completing this learning activity. Solutions to some or all of the problems will be presented and discussed during the course of the applied class.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
